speaker: interviewer
text: 16th of May, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia. Rolling. And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me how it was to recommit to life in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a strange thing. I came back. And because my involvement in sport, I fitted into life practically immediately. I met a few friend of mine-- school friend of mine who survived, and then I started playing soccer in a little club, which was the only Jewish club in Hungary, called [NON-ENGLISH] which was the Hungarian section of the famous Hakoah-Vienna sport organization-- entirely Jewish, run by Jews and played by Jews-- football team, which played in the third division in Hungary. And I was 17, physically back-- 18, 17, physically back to normal. And I start playing soccer in the third division, first as a junior and then as a third-division player. And I didn't work until I become 18. Then an uncle of mine, who was a silversmith by trade, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't have money. My parents did. And they established a partnership. He opened a silversmith shop. And I started learning silversmithing as an apprentice. And I even got the-- the-- the apprentice letter that I am a silversmith. But I was never-- because I spend more time in training and more time in football than in-- in the shop. And my parents wasn't happy. So cut the story short, they lost a lot of money in the venture. I never became a silversmith. I became a footballer. And my parents closed the business down. And then, in 1948, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to play a trial game for a team which become a first-division team. They had won the second division in Hungary, and they automatically qualified for first division, which-- you have to know, those days in Hungary, playing first-division soccer was like today, you are Magic Johnson. Because from 1948 till 1956, the Hungarian soccer was ten miles above every other footballing nation in the world That includes England, Brazil, Uruguay, and whatever. And we had a 10-year period of soccer-- and I'm not public relationing for it. But it has a 10 years where you could find a world-class footballer in every corner in Budapest. And you could buy them for sixpence a dozen. That's how it was, the standard in football. So those days, to play first-division soccer in Hungary was a tremendously big achievement. And I went for a trial. And I became a member of a team which belonged to a huge textile factory. And the Hungarian sports those days were communist, which meant that, on paper, you were not professional. On real life, you were professional. You were more professional than a professional, because you had such a power as a well-known footballer that, if your friend was in jail, you could go and get him out. Professional players in the west couldn't do that. They give you a job, like you became a time analyst without knowing how to add up two and two. But that was the only way they could pay you. So the Hungarian sport could appear on every Olympic games where professional athletes was competing with amateurs. And our status in the world sport was tremendous. I mean, gold medals in Olympic games in Hungary-- even in 1936 in Berlin, they had 10 gold medals. And they run third in the world among the nation. And from then on-- in Helsinki '14, in London '16, and whatever it is, and even in Melbourne when only a skeleton staff came, they collected quite a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I became a professional footballer. Or you could call it a sham amateur. I had a job in the factory. But I never went, only first of the month to pick up the wages. And I played soccer. I played soccer successfully. But that team was only a promoted team from second division to first division. So next year, because I was successful, one of the most famous clubs in Hungary, called MTK-- which was run by Jewish people, sponsored by Jewish people, but were no Jewish footballers. And I become member of the team until '48, end of '48, it becomes a textile union team because no more private teams existed. Hungary and all the top teams joined either to the workers-- the iron workers union, which was Vasas, or MTK, which was textiles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ferencvaros became the meat workers. Ujpest-- that was the four most famous teams in Hungary. They became the police force team. And they all get titles-- lieutenant. You went into-- I went into the army Monday as a draftee. And Wednesday, I was a lieutenant. So that's express you my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I played for MTK in the '48-'49 season till '50. And I went with the-- with-- with MTK to Czechoslovakia because it used to be a competition between the two top Czech team and the two top Hungarian team, what we called the Easter Cup. At Easter was a cup, two days. One day, one played against the other and then the other one. The winner took the trophy. And that was going on for 30 or 40 years. And was a very famous cup in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I played there. And my Auschwitz time came back once more. We stayed in a hotel called Zlata Housa, which is The Golden Goose. And in Kaufbeuren in [NON-ENGLISH], we had a [GERMAN], an older Czech Jew who was very nice. He was just looking after the barracks. And I know him very well. And a football team, when it goes on trip, do two things-- sorry, three-- eat, play billiards, and play cards. After eating, you go for a walk. And we walked on Wenzelsplatz, which is the heart of Prague. And with us walked at least another 15,000 people. Because that's the Sunday stroll, or Saturday stroll-- that's the promenade in-- in Prague. And we go out for walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And even today I can't believe it. In about 10 minutes walking, who doesn't come opposite me but this Czech guy who was my [GERMAN] in Kaufbeuren, [NON-ENGLISH]. If you asked me his name, if I die I don't know. And he didn't recognize me. Because from 15 years old, I became 18 years old, 19 years old. From a skeleton, I became a footballer with a world-famous team. But he only grayed. And his face was so front of me, like all the faces I met during my-- my German trip, or castration? Not castration, it's another word for it-- castration or-- because if I say trip, maybe people think it was a pleasure trip. So he came and I said, I think so, Pavel, or Pavlov, or Heinrich or Havlech, I don't know. And he looked at me. He said, who are you? I said, Jules, from Kaufbeuren. And he did [CLAP]. He said, [SPEAKING YIDDISH] because he was Czech, but he spoke Yiddish. I only spoke German. If he would've speak Czech to me, I wouldn't understand a word. And he said in Yiddish, he said, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. What are you doing here? How come? I told him I'm with-- and he said he's going to the match. I said, OK. Then you come with me. So he came with me. He Came to the dressing room. I introduced to him the friends. And we stayed only two days. So the world, as huge as it is, I had to bump into that person in Prague. And from then on, I became a first-division footballer with MTK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came 1950. When you are 20, in Hungary you go to the Army. And I had to appeal to the front of a committee. They said, you go to here. And they didn't tell us. They told the wagon because we weren't exactly back, again, the same wagon like we went in Auschwitz with. This time, they didn't close the door. But from Budapest, they took us to a 200-kilometer country town, to an army depot where I started my army life-- hair off, hair off, and back to Auschwitz again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to myself, hell, how the hell am I going to stand this? MTK promised me that I'm coming out from the army immediately. And I appeared there. They're fixing up in the ministerium that I got out. They couldn't because in the second-largest city-- in Budapest, the Honved, which is the army team, they got knowing that I am moved down to that place. And they fixed up in the ministerium that I have to join the army team in that city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from a little, bald-headed recruit on Monday, I became a lieutenant on Wednesday and then started three years of my army life, which was more beautiful than anything you can imagine. Because when you are a top sportsman in Hungary, you live like a king. And after three years, when I finished my army, I went back and I joined a football team which was the largest electrical appliance makers in Europe, called Tingstrom. And even in Australia, you find electric bulbs which is comes from there. And I became a footballer. I became a time analyst. And I have no idea what time is. And I played there until the revolution break out. And during the revolution-- revolution, I had two experiences which made me to leave. Because, as a footballer, I lived extremely well in Hungary. Number one experience was that, during the revolution, I wake up. I look out the window, and I look into 20 guns. And when I pull my head back, they start shooting the windows. And in Europe, when something happens, customarily everybody goes down to the cellars. Air raid, shooting, whatever, everybody down the cellars. So we were going down the cellars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the caretaker comes down and said, Jules, they're looking for you. So I go up to the empty house in the open place, stand 20 young nobodies with guns from bottom to top. And one of them said, you shot at us from your window. And I said, my God, what do you mean I shot? I have no guns. I just woke up, went to the mirror in between the two windows to have a look at myself. He said, you bloody liar so and so. You shot at us and that and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, fortunately, they didn't kill me on the spot. Because those days, life in Hungary was one second-- 1/10 of a second, somebody wants to shoot you, they shoot you. Nobody asks questions. They-- they said-- one of them said, let's go up and have a look at his flat. So they came up to the flat. And, thank God again, I had footballing pictures on my flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of them looked around and looked at pictures. And he said, you was playing for [NON-ENGLISH] Honved? I said yes. And he said to the leader, hey, I know this guy. He said, they're a footballer. They were no army nothing. He said, we call them chocolate soldiers. And the guy answered, if they didn't shoot it from here, maybe they shoot it from somewhere else. And they left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for a day, I couldn't eat, I couldn't sleep, because my life was-- when I went there and they said I shoot at them, I died. I knew that my life maybe last another two seconds. And then I said to my wife, Mommy, get the child. We're getting out. She said, you're not going anywhere. I said, then I go by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1956, November. And she said, let's go out to your people's place, to your parents, and ask them what we're supposed to do. I said, OK. I'll give you that much. In those days, to go out-- was no train, no nothing. Life in Budapest was standstill because of the revolution. So we walked out to my parents' place. And I said, where is Mommy? She said, she's out in the market. She moved since then to a different market, very close where-- where we lived in our new home. And I said, all right. I'll go and pick her up. So I go out to the market. And all I seen, about a few hundred people in front of my mother's stall screaming that the bloody Jews-- she pinched the goose from the people of the communist republic, and so forth. And again, the same day or maybe the next day, I see again people armed from bottom to top-- bazooka, hand grenade, machine guns, you name it-- in front of my mother. And I couldn't go into-- into the back of the shop, because I saw my mother white like a sheet between two armed guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked the guy, what happened to my mother? He said, the bloody Jew was selling goods which was sent from the country to the capital city as a present for money. And I said, this Jewing start all over again. And one of the leaders of this mob said he'd take the woman into the school, nearby school, to interrogate her. And my mother-- when she came out, I said, Mommy, what happened? She said, I don't know. I'm a member of a-- of a-- how you call when a lot of people organizing? Commune, like a commune, but a business commune. She said, I got the goods from the distributor. I have to pay for it to the distributor. And I'm selling it. I'm not my own. This is not my business. That business belongs to the combine. But the people wouldn't listen. They said, where is your papers? She said, the distributor has it. And one of them was decent enough. We don't hurt her, he said, until we find the distributor. So my mother was, for two days, in the school. And in those days, to find somebody-- with no transportation, everybody in the move, revolution is on-- to find somebody's a miracle. And the miracle, they find the person. And he came into the school, and he produced the invoices-- black and white-- that my mother was responsible for what he bought.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the what we called [GERMAN] which is an expression that I learned us a child-- they are the-- the working class who never worked in his life. He lives his life in the pub. And all he does is spreads rumors and whatever he can against the other one so he get a free beer. He went to the shop. And he said, look at this bloody Jew. She's selling goose for money, which you're not supposed to-- in a-- in a hope that, in the big smuzzle, he might grab something out of it. And that was it. I said to my wife, you pack. My mother came out two days after. And I said, Mommy, Daddy, you pack, too. My sister and my in-laws, they were living there temporarily because they came from Romania. And we all go. And my father and my mother said, we're too old to go. They were, at that time-- 1956, they were-- my father was 57 years of age. He said, we are too old to leave. You go. I don't hold it against you, and I don't blame you. You go. And my wife, my daughter, my sister, and my brother-in-law and her son, we went to the borderline in order to be able to get through. Because Hungary was a no-man's land. There was no Russians and was no Hungarians. It was a no-government situation. And one of the peasants in the field wanted to take us over to Austria. But the 20, 25-kilometer walk was so difficult he didn't want to come. So he showed me the stars, which way to go. And we were going through bushes, unbelievable, for 25 kilometers with a daughter on the arm and about 150 people behind me all going to Austria. And apart from us, it was about another 150,000 people who left Hungary in that time in certain different places going to Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrived safely, bloodied because the pricks in the-- in the bushes cut everything off-- socks, and clothes, and whatever. But we arrived to Austria. And-- and in the hotel, we got from the joint, which was the World Jewish Organization, some money in order to be able to stay in a hotel in Austria. In those days, the revolution, the refugees was helped by every country. Australia helped. England helped, France and the United States, and everybody. And they gave quote to certain countries, certain amount of people could go immediately without visas or whatever. So in the hotel-- actually, in Vienna, I met all my other cousins who, since then, left Hungary. So five cousins met in a hotel and put five nations into a hat, where to go. And I said, you put your things into the hat and pick. I go to Australia because, with international football, I was in Red China. I was in Moscow. And I was very close to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in my childhood, I read about sharks and whatever a child reads about it. And I said, I'm going to Australia. You pick whatever you want. You go wherever you wanted to go. So they take the things out of the hat, and everybody decided to come to Australia. All we had to do was go to the Australian embassy and apply for coming to Australia free of charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived 1957, 1st of February, after a six-week trip from Vienna to Genova, and from Genova by ship to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reception like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The reception like-- if I tell you, it's unbelievable. We arrive in the ship to Melbourne. And I didn't know anybody. And nobody knew anybody. We had some skirmishes on the ship because a little bit of anti-Semitism break out. But this time, we know what is it. So it was 20 Jews or 40 Jews standing in one corner, and 20 or 40 Hungarians in the other one. And we said, come over if you want something. And nobody came. So the trip was quite good.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we came to Melbourne, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they wanted to take us to Bonegilla because that's where all the refugees came. And I said, look, I'm a footballer. It must be some football here, too. Why do I go to Bonegilla? And as I'm thinking, a voice came from downstairs. It said, Jules Forgacs, Jules Forgacs? And I shouted down, yes? That's-- my name is so and so. The Hakoah from Melbourne sent me out here to take you off the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody here got note that I was on the list of the ship in Melbourne, and they know I played for MTK. And they wanted players. So I said, yes, but I haven't got anywhere to go. So the guy said, I'll fix it up. You wait. And I waited, and waited, and waited. He didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And suddenly another voice. Excuse me, have you got where to go? I said no. He said, I'll come up and pick you up, a man I never saw in my life. And he came up. His name is-- he was-- he died. He was a Doctor Kolinger. He was a chemical engineer. And he took me-- sorry, my wife, the child, and me to his home in Highett, in Victoria, Melbourne.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know a word of English. I didn't know what city. I know I'm in Melbourne, but that was about it. I knew nothing about Australia. And he took me in. And I stayed there for three weeks until Hakoah find me. And then I started playing soccer there. And from then on, started my Australian way of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a question about your name change-- when did that happen, from Fischer to Forgacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My parents was very upset about our German names, because of the German happenings. So when I came home from Dachau in '47-- actually, I sent this gentlemen, Miklaus, to look for Andras Fischer, or Bonde Fischer. And he couldn't find it. But he asked a gentlemen named Fishcer, had a poultry shop here. And one of the sellers said, you want Forgacs Bonde. And he said, I don't know. I want Fischer Bonde. He was Fischer. He's Forgacs now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how we changed. But when I came back, the name on the shop was already Forgacs. And I asked my mother, what is this Forgacs business? And she said, while you were away, by deeds of which I have papers here, you are Jules Forgacs from now on, because we don't want to be known as Fischer anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what happened. They went to the authorities, whoever that section belonged to. And by deeds, by law they changed their name to Fischer. And I never read Fischer anymore. And only my childhood friends here, with whom I went to school-- I have a couple of them who knows me as Fischer. But my name is Forgacs. And everybody knows me by Forgacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling and camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what happened, then, in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then they took me from Doctor Kolinger-- Hakoah. And I played a couple of games in Hakoah at Melbourne. And then we came to a friendly game, to Sydney, in about six, seven weeks after my Melbourne staying. In those days, the Australian Federation formed and broke away from the Association, which meant that Hakoah could get me from Melbourne without any clearance whatsoever. Because Melbourne was-- and still is-- one section of the Australian Soccer Association. And the Federation formed by Hakoah with all the top teams joined them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came to play Hakoah. And I was a Hakoah member for about three years, four years. And Hakoah helped me tremendously. In Melbourne, I had a job as a-- as a laborer in an Abattoir. In Hakoah, I was an electric plater for a couple of weeks. And when my English started picking up, I got a job from a tremendously nice gentleman who died next to me on the dancing floor in a Hakoah club. And I was a company representative for a printing firm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1960, I bought a taxicab because I made enough money from football to buy a cab. And I was a cabbie from 1960 to '71, which made me even more street wise than I was before. And I had a tremendous experience in 10 years. But in that 10 years, I played football for about three or four years. And the rest of them, I retired. I became a coach of Auburn, the football club, which was top division then. And then in 1971, my cab was involved in an accident where three people died. And I just couldn't do it anymore. So I sold my taxicab. It was in the night time, and my night driver had injury, and so forth. They told me he died. But, fortunately, I find him in the emergency room. Nobody could recognize him because of the gauze around his face. That's why they thought he died. And the next day, I sold-- I went to the commission, and I said, I want to sell my cab. And then Hakoah connection was working again. Actually, I played for Panhellenic, which is the former club for Olympic-- which is now Olympic, the Greek club, which was also tremendously good to me financially, and connectionally, and mentally, as well. And in '71, one the Hakoah directors offered me a business because he was joining the Hakoah club in such a way that he'd become general manager of the then-builded club, which was built by Frank Lowy of Westfield fame. And they, both of them, established Hakoah. So he sold his business, which is-- was a coffee roasting plant and importation of coffee and supplying coffee to outlets like espressos, coffee lunches, and the others. And I owned that for 20, 22 years. I owned my business, which helped me to establish myself, not just like a footballer, as a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the same times, my daughter got married to a five-generation Aussie. And I have three grandsons. One of them is a budding actor. One of them is the Australian Schoolboy Table Tennis Champion, and the little one is the Australian Schoolboy Junior-- New Southwest Schoolboy Champion. And-- and we live happily ever after. I don't think so-- I can tell you any much that you-- you're looking at contented cow. Enough? Yeah. What was that thing-- the contented cow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cow, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to retract that statement, Jules? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me when I was-- just before my bar mitzvah-- 13, early 13. That's 1942. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Budapest, made by photographers who made 200 million photos a day in one of the-- one of the parks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me in 1949 in Prague, playing against-- I am under the textiles shirt. And it is in Prague playing against one of the greatest right wingers in the world, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me again, my first appearance in the newspapers in Budapest in 1949. And when we played-- MTK played the first very, very important game against Kispest, which was the champion. And we ran second in the competition.

speaker: interviewer
text: This one, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Eva and myself in 1993. On the-- on the-- I think, so the occasion is somebody's birthday. We went. And this is my home where the picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my immediate family-- my daughter Agnes, Mark, Richie, Stephen and Paul Brandon. And this was taken when my grandson bar mitzvah-- the oldest son, Stephen. And it was at the after temple Emmanuel when the reception was finished. And all I want to say is that, when I look at these three boys, that I'm absolutely delighted that they didn't have to and won't have to go through the things what me and all my fellow Jews had to go in the past. And I hope their life will be happier than mine. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape six of the interview with Jules Forgacs. It's the 16th of May, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. And that's the end of the whole interview.
